description: Mexico's boho-chic playground — clifftop Mayan ruins overlooking Caribbean turquoise, cenote swimming in cathedral-like caves, eco-chic beach clubs, and a wellness scene that has somehow merged ancient rituals with Instagram aesthetics.

neighborhoodRecs: Tulum Beach (Zona Hotelera) — The famous beach strip, eco-hotels, beach clubs, cenote access. Expensive but what you came for.
Tulum Town (Pueblo) — Where locals and budget travellers stay, 3km from beach, restaurants and supermarkets, very affordable.
La Veleta — New development between town and beach, growing restaurant scene, more accessible prices.
areasToAvoid: Walking alone on the road between town and beach at night — sporadic lighting, occasional incidents.
Sargassum seaweed months (May–August) can heavily affect some beaches — check current conditions before booking.
Overly remote cenotes without a guide — some involve cliff jumps or deep dives that require knowledge of the site.
scamWarnings: Cenote ticket touts: some cenotes have unofficial sellers near the entrance charging above the official rate. Go to the official booth.
Taxi price gouging from Cancún airport — always pre-book or use the official ADO bus (MXN $200 to Tulum, 2 hours) from CUN.
Fake shaman ceremonies charging hundreds of dollars — legitimate Mayan ceremonies are community events, not $300 tourist packages.
Beach club minimum spends not clearly stated — confirm the minimum spend and what's included before sitting down.

airportTransfer.cheapest: ADO bus from Cancún airport directly to Tulum bus station — MXN $200–220, takes 2–2.5 hours. Most reliable option.
airportTransfer.fastest: Private transfer Cancún airport to Tulum Beach — MXN $1,200–1,800 ($60–90), takes 1.5 hours.
airportTransfer.recommended: ADO bus for solo and couple travellers. Private for families or groups of 4+ splitting the cost.
airportTransfer.taxiEstimate: Official pre-paid taxi booth in Cancún airport: MXN $1,800–2,200 to Tulum. Avoid unofficial drivers outside the terminal.

tippingEtiquette: 10–15% at restaurants is expected. Beach clubs: more generous tipping gets you better service.
Cenote tour guides: MXN $100–200 per person tip is appropriate.
Yoga class/retreat instructors: MXN $50–100 tip if you felt it was valuable.
Tulum has embraced digital nomad culture — English is widely spoken and tipping norms are US-influenced.
Eco-hotel staff who handle luggage: MXN $50–100 per bag tip is appreciated in remote beachfront hotels.

dailyCostBreakdown.accommodation: Town hostel MXN $300–500 | Town guesthouse MXN $600–1,200 | Beach cabana MXN $2,500–6,000 | Eco-hotel MXN $4,000–12,000
dailyCostBreakdown.meals: Town taqueria MXN $60–100 | Beach café breakfast MXN $150–250 | Beach restaurant dinner MXN $350–700
dailyCostBreakdown.transport: Bicycle rental MXN $100–150/day | Collectivo (shared taxi) MXN $30 to Cobá | Taxi town-beach MXN $100–150
dailyCostBreakdown.activities: Tulum ruins MXN $85 | Gran Cenote entry MXN $380 | Snorkelling cenote tour MXN $800–1,200 | Kite surf lesson MXN $1,500

packingTips: Biodegradable sunscreen — required to enter most cenotes to protect the ecosystem. Non-biodegradable is confiscated.
Light layers for evenings — the sea breeze can be genuinely cool December–February.
Sandals with ankle support — the jungle paths to cenotes are rough.
Reusable water bottle — tap water is unsafe, single-use plastic is destructive to this environment.
premium.bookingStrategy: Book beach accommodation 2–3 months ahead for December–April. Last-minute rates in June–October (rainy/sargassum season) can be 40–50% lower. Cenote visits are best Tuesday–Thursday when weekend crowds are absent.
localTips: Gran Cenote is beautiful and open early — arrive before 9am to swim in crystal clarity before the tour groups arrive.
Tulum ruins are best at opening time (8am) — you'll have 30 minutes before tour buses from Cancún arrive.
Cook dinner once at a rental kitchen — beach restaurant prices are genuinely very high.
Rent a bicycle instead of taxis for the town-to-beach journey — saves MXN $100–150 each way and takes just 20 min.
Sargassum tracker websites (Google 'Tulum sargassum forecast') let you check seaweed conditions for your travel dates.
premium.bestTimeToVisit: November–April: dry season, clear turquoise water, no seaweed, sea turtles nesting (May–Oct). February–March is the sweet spot before spring break crowds.
dayTrips: Cobá — 45 min by collectivo, climbable Mayan pyramid (one of the last in Mexico you can still climb), jungle setting.
Sian Ka'an Biosphere Reserve — 30 min south, UNESCO wetlands, sea turtles, manatees, crocodiles, boat tour essential.
Bacalar Lagoon — 2 hours south, 'Lake of Seven Colors', sailing, kayaking, colonial fort, much quieter than Tulum.
visaInfo: Citizens of the US, UK, EU countries, Canada, and Australia: no visa required for tourist stays up to 180 days — passport on arrival is sufficient
A tourist card (FMM — Forma Migratoria Múltiple) is issued on arrival at Cancún International Airport (CUN) and must be kept throughout your stay and surrendered on departure
Tulum is reached from Cancún Airport — 1.5–2 hours by car or shuttle; no separate immigration at Tulum
Mexico's 180-day tourist stay is one of the most generous in the world — no visa or extension process required for most Western nationalities
Airlines may request an onward ticket at check-in — book a flexible return to present if asked
currencyTips: Currency: Mexican Peso (MXN / $). Tulum is one of Mexico's most expensive tourist destinations — upscale beach clubs and restaurants are priced at premium levels
USD widely accepted in Tulum's tourist zone (La Zona Hotelera) at roughly the market rate; MXN gives marginally better value at local restaurants and colectivos
Best ATMs: BBVA and Banorte ATMs in Tulum Pueblo (town) are most reliable for foreign cards; beach zone ATMs charge higher fees
Cash is essential for cenotes (most charge MXN entry), colectivos (shared vans — cash only), and local taquerias in Tulum Pueblo
Most upscale beach clubs and hotels in La Zona Hotelera accept cards; confirm before assuming
premium.simCard: Telcel has the best coverage in and around Tulum, the Riviera Maya, and the Yucatán Peninsula. AT&T Mexico is an alternative. SIMs are available at Cancún Airport (CUN) and at Oxxo convenience stores in Tulum Pueblo. A 30-day Telcel SIM with 30 GB costs MXN 200–350. Mobile signal is limited at more remote cenotes and jungle areas — download offline maps in advance. eSIM via Airalo is available.

emergencyNumbers.emergency: 911 (national emergency number) / 078 (tourist assistance line in Quintana Roo)
